range_notes: Migrates through southern Alberta; breeds where milkweeds occur.
description: Iconic migratory butterfly. Caterpillars feed only on milkweeds (Asclepias). Listed as Endangered under SARA in Canada.

range_notes: Widespread across boreal and aspen parkland Alberta.
description: Large yellow-and-black swallowtail. Caterpillars feed on aspen, birch, willow, and chokecherry.

range_notes: Southwestern Alberta foothills and river valleys.
description: Closely related to the Canadian Tiger Swallowtail. Caterpillars feed on willow, cottonwood, and chokecherry.
Yellow-and-black swallowtail of open prairie. Prairie subspecies caterpillars feed on Artemisia (wild sages).

range_notes: Common throughout Alberta wherever willows occur.
description: Dark butterfly with yellow wing borders; one of the first to emerge in spring. Caterpillars feed on willow, poplar, birch, and elm.

range_notes: Common in moist meadows and disturbed ground.
description: Orange-banded tortoiseshell butterfly. Caterpillars feed exclusively on stinging nettle (Urtica).
Mottled brown-and-orange tortoiseshell. Caterpillars feed on birch, aspen, and willow.
image_attribution: (c) D. Gordon E. Robertson, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

range_notes: Periodic immigrant; major irruptions every several years.
description: Cosmopolitan migrant butterfly. Caterpillars are generalists on Asteraceae, especially thistles.
Dark butterfly with red wing-bands. Caterpillars feed on stinging nettle.
image_attribution: (c) Gilles San Martin, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Gilles San Martin

description: Black butterfly with broad white wing-bands. Caterpillars feed on aspen, willow, and birch.

range_notes: Meadows and openings across central and southern Alberta.
description: Orange fritillary butterfly. Caterpillars feed only on native violets (Viola).
Smaller orange fritillary of higher elevations. Caterpillars feed only on violets.
Brown butterfly with eye-spots. Caterpillars feed on native grasses.
Tiny pale-blue butterfly. Caterpillars feed on flower buds of dogwoods, viburnums, and cherries.
Small blue butterfly with silvery underside. Caterpillars feed on lupines, vetches, and milkvetches.
Small tailed-blue butterfly. Caterpillars feed on legume seedpods (milkvetch, vetch).
Orange-and-brown skipper. Caterpillars feed on native bunchgrasses.
Large silk moth with eye-spots on hind wings. Caterpillars feed on birch, aspen, willow, and cherry.
image_attribution: (c) Trevor Van Loon, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Trevor Van Loon

description: Largest North American silk moth. Caterpillars feed on willow, cherry, birch, and maple.
Hovers like a hummingbird at flowers. Caterpillars feed on honeysuckle and snowberry.
Black-and-yellow day-flying moth. Caterpillars feed on snowberry and honeysuckle.

range_notes: Widespread breeder; nomadic in winter following fruit.
description: Sleek crested songbird that feeds primarily on fruit. Strongly associated with serviceberry, mountain ash, and viburnum.
Bright yellow seed-eating finch. Heavily dependent on Asteraceae seed (sunflower, thistle, goldenrod, aster).

range_notes: Year-round in boreal regions; irruptive elsewhere.
description: Streaked finch that feeds on birch, alder, and conifer seeds.

range_notes: Winter visitor; breeds in the far north.
description: Small streaky finch with red cap. Winter diet is dominated by birch and alder seeds.

range_notes: Summer breeder in central and northern Alberta.
description: Tiny migratory hummingbird. Feeds at red tubular flowers; bee balm and columbine are major nectar sources.
Aggressive coppery hummingbird of the mountain west. Feeds at red tubular flowers.
Insectivorous songbird; nestlings depend almost entirely on caterpillars. Native trees rich in lepidoptera (aspen, willow, birch, cherry) anchor its food supply.

range_notes: Summer breeder; strongly tied to riparian willow and dogwood thickets.
description: Bright yellow warbler that nests almost exclusively in willow and dogwood shrubs along streams and wetland edges.
Familiar thrush. Eats earthworms in spring/summer; switches to fruit (serviceberry, mountain ash, chokecherry, hawthorn) in fall and winter.
Large social bees critical for cold-climate pollination. Generalist foragers; particularly important on lupines, bergamot, asters, and goldenrods.

range_notes: Diverse genus with many Alberta species.
description: Solitary ground-nesting bees; many are spring specialists tied to willow catkins, early native shrubs, and asters.
Solitary bees that nest in cavities (bee hotels). Important pollinators of fruit trees and shrubs.
Solitary bees that cut leaf disks to line nests. Strong foragers on legumes and composites.
Small to medium ground-nesting bees, often metallic green. Highly generalist on composites and other prairie flowers.
Common prairie/parkland bumble bee; long colony season makes it a key generalist pollinator of asters, clovers and mints.
Orange-banded bumble bee of boreal and parkland edges; forages willow, blueberry, goldenrod.
Highly variable short-tongued bumble bee common in gardens and grassland.
Long-tongued bumble bee favouring deep flowers (legumes, mints).
Spring solitary bee; superb fruit-tree and willow pollinator that nests in cavities (and bee hotels).
Solitary cavity-nester that cuts leaf discs (often from rose) to line its nest cells; strong summer pollinator of legumes and asters.
image_attribution: (c) Owen Strickland, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Owen Strickland

description: Metallic-green communal ground-nesting bee; generalist on open flowers.
Hugely diverse genus of tiny ground-nesting generalist pollinators — among the most abundant bees in any Alberta garden.
Early ground-nesting bee that lines burrows with a cellophane-like secretion; vital willow and early-spring pollinator.
Social sweat bee nesting in bare soil; generalist on composites.
Fast-flying solitary digger bee; long tongue suits penstemons and mints.
image_attribution: (c) Amy Schnebelin, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Amy Schnebelin

description: Open-country raptor that nests in tall poplars and spruce and hunts small mammals over meadows.
Alberta's provincial bird; nests in poplar/spruce, controls rodents.
Small falcon that reuses old crow/magpie nests in spruce; hunts small birds in towns and parkland.
Cavity-nesting little falcon of open country; eats grasshoppers, voles.
Smallest woodpecker; forages insects on shrubs and trees and excavates nest cavities in soft dead wood (poplar, birch).
Ground-foraging woodpecker that eats ants and autumn fruit; nests in aspen/poplar snags.
Bold parkland corvid; caches acorns and conifer seeds and takes fruit.
image_attribution: (c) Judy Gallagher, some rights reserved (CC BY)

description: Ubiquitous corvid; omnivore whose bulky thorny nests shelter other species after use.
Boreal/foothills jay that caches food in spruce; year-round resident.
Conifer specialist that eats spruce/pine seeds and bark insects; nests in cavities.
Shrub-and-edge sparrow; eats grass and forb seeds and nests low in dense cover.
Ground-feeding sparrow of forest edges; winters on fallen weed seeds.
Large winter finch that feeds on mountain-ash and other persistent fruit plus conifer and birch seeds.
Winter-flocking frugivore that strips berries from saskatoon, highbush-cranberry, hawthorn and rose.
Aspen-woodland grouse that eats poplar/willow buds, catkins and shrub fruit; broods need insect-rich understory.
Honey-bee-mimicking hover fly (introduced from Europe, now widespread); adults are major generalist pollinators of composites and umbels.
Hover fly whose aphid-eating larvae are valuable biocontrol; adults take pollen and nectar.
Tiny hover fly; aphid-predator larvae, pollinator adults.
Native lady beetle; both adults and larvae eat aphids, adults also take pollen and nectar.
Declining native lady beetle; aphid predator of grassland and gardens.
'Aphid lions' as larvae; adults sip nectar and honeydew. Key garden biocontrol.
Night-active predators of slugs, cutworms and weed seeds; shelter in leaf litter and groundcover.
Pollen- and nectar-feeding beetle of late-summer goldenrod; larvae are soil predators.
Large migratory dragonfly; aerial predator of mosquitoes and flies, needs ponds with emergent sedges.
Pond dragonfly of marsh edges; voracious insect predator.
Late-season red dragonfly of wet meadows and pond margins.
Slender damselfly of vegetated still water; larvae and adults prey on small insects.
Insectivorous bat eating thousands of mosquitoes nightly; roosts under bark and in tree cavities. SARA-listed (Endangered).
Hardy urban-tolerant bat; forages beetles and moths over meadows and water.
Migratory tree bat roosting under bark of poplar and spruce.
Abundant native seed-eater and key prey for owls, hawks and foxes; disperses seeds and fungi.
Grassland herbivore whose runways shape meadows; cornerstone prey for raptors and weasels.
Tiny voracious insectivore of moist litter; eats insects, slugs and larvae.
Day-and-dusk hawkmoth; a hummingbird-like nectar pollinator of tubular flowers; larvae feed on flax and evening-primrose family plants.
Large hawkmoth whose caterpillars feed on poplar and willow foliage.
Eyed hawkmoth; larvae host on willow, birch and cherry.
The 'woolly bear' caterpillar; a generalist on asters, goldenrods and many forbs that overwinters as a larva.
Tussock caterpillar feeding on willow, poplar, alder and birch.
image_attribution: (c) Paul Bedell, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by Paul Bedell

description: Large skipper; caterpillars host on native legumes (milkvetch, vetch).
Abundant grassland butterfly; caterpillars feed on native grasses.
Ubiquitous yellow butterfly; caterpillars host on legumes (vetch, milkvetch, lupine).
Prairie blue whose caterpillars feed on legumes and are tended by ants.
Small orange-and-black butterfly; caterpillars host on native asters.
